1:
"And if a soul sin, and hear the voice of swearing, and is a witness, whether he hath seen or known of it; if he do not utter it, then he shall bear his iniquity." The witnesses note the sin of concealing testimony under adjuration. On "if a soul sin, and hear the voice of swearing, and is a witness... if he do not utter it," the grammatical voices note the case. Henry and Gill note that "if a soul sin, and hear the voice of swearing, and is a witness, whether he hath seen or known of it; if he do not utter it, then he shall bear his iniquity" — if a man, hearing the voice of adjuration (a public charge or oath put to witnesses to testify), being a witness who had seen or known the matter, did not utter (testify) it, he sinned and bore his iniquity; the adjuration (the public oath calling on witnesses to speak, Matthew 26:63); the concealing (the witness staying silent, suppressing the truth); the guilt (the sin of silence, the duty to testify neglected).

On "hear the voice of swearing, and is a witness," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the duty. Calvin and Ellicott note the witness under adjuration (bound to testify, the truth required) — the duty (the obligation to bear witness to the truth, not to conceal it); the lesson (the sin of suppressing truth, the duty of honest testimony, the guilt of silence when called to speak, Proverbs 29:24).

On "if he do not utter it, then he shall bear his iniquity," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the guilt: "then he shall bear his iniquity" — the bearing of the guilt (the sin of concealment, the iniquity borne until atoned); the figure (the sins of omission, the neglect of duty, needing atonement); the providence (the trespass offering for the sin of silence, vv.5-6). The verse displays the sin of concealing testimony under adjuration.

The witnesses agree the substance: the witness who, adjured, concealed his testimony, sinned and bore his iniquity (Henry, Calvin, Wesley, Gill). The verse bears its weight: the sin of concealing testimony — the witness under public adjuration bound to testify (Matthew 26:63), the guilt of suppressing the truth, the sin of silence when called to speak (Proverbs 29:24), a sin of omission and neglected duty needing atonement (the trespass offering, vv.5-6). The verse displays the concealment. The verse marks the witness: silent. The verse displays the truth: suppressed. The verse displays the guilt: borne. The verse displays the duty: neglected. The concealment displays the sin of suppressing the truth. The verse displays the witness: guilty. The verse displays the silence: sinful. The verse displays the duty: to testify. The sin of concealing testimony under adjuration displays the guilt of silence: if he do not utter it, he shall bear his iniquity (Matthew 26:63; Proverbs 29:24) — the witness bound to testify, the sin of concealing the truth, a sin of omission needing atonement. The verse displays the sin of touching the unclean (v.2).
2:
"Or if a soul touch any unclean thing, whether it be a carcase of an unclean beast, or a carcase of unclean cattle, or the carcase of unclean creeping things, and if it be hidden from him; he also shall be unclean, and guilty." The very brief verse notes the sin of touching the unclean unawares. The grammatical voices note the uncleanness. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "if a soul touch any unclean thing, whether it be a carcase of an unclean beast... and if it be hidden from him; he also shall be unclean, and guilty" — if a man touched an unclean thing (the carcase of an unclean beast, cattle, or creeping thing), even unawares (hidden from him), he became unclean and guilty; the touching (the contact with the unclean, defiling); the hidden (done unawares, in ignorance); the guilt (the uncleanness contracted, the guilt real, needing cleansing and atonement).

On "if a soul touch any unclean thing," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the defilement. Calvin and Ellicott note the touching of the unclean (the defilement, the ceremonial uncleanness) — the figure (the defilement of sin, the contact with what is unclean, the pollution of the world, 2 Corinthians 6:17; the need of cleansing).

On "if it be hidden from him; he also shall be unclean, and guilty," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the guilt: "if it be hidden from him; he also shall be unclean, and guilty" — the uncleanness even unawares (the guilt real, though hidden); the figure (the defilement of unknown sin, the conscience defiled, the need of cleansing in Christ, 1 John 1:7); the providence (the trespass offering for the uncleanness, the cleansing provided). The verse displays the sin of touching the unclean unawares.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the uncleanness — "if a soul touch any unclean thing... and if it be hidden from him; he also shall be unclean, and guilty," the defilement (Keil, Gill); "if a soul touch any unclean thing," the defilement (2 Corinthians 6:17, Calvin, Ellicott); "if it be hidden from him; he also shall be unclean, and guilty," the guilt (1 John 1:7, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the unclean. The verse marks the touching: the unclean. The verse displays the hidden: unawares. The verse displays the uncleanness: contracted. The verse displays the cleansing: needed. The touching displays the defilement of the unclean. The verse displays the contact: defiling. The verse displays the guilt: real. The verse displays the Christ: who cleanses. The sin of touching the unclean unawares displays the defilement: he also shall be unclean, and guilty (2 Corinthians 6:17; 1 John 1:7) — the defilement of the unclean even unawares, the conscience defiled, the need of cleansing in Christ. The verse displays the sin of touching human uncleanness (v.3).
3:
"Or if he touch the uncleanness of man, whatsoever uncleanness it be that a man shall be defiled withal, and it be hid from him; when he knoweth of it, then he shall be guilty." The very brief verse notes the sin of touching human uncleanness. The grammatical voices note the defilement. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "if he touch the uncleanness of man, whatsoever uncleanness it be that a man shall be defiled withal, and it be hid from him; when he knoweth of it, then he shall be guilty" — if a man touched any human uncleanness (the various defilements of man, Leviticus 12-15), even unawares, when he came to know it he was guilty; the human uncleanness (the defilements of the body, disease, issues, death); the hidden (done unawares); the knowing (when the sin came to his knowledge, the guilt acknowledged, vv.5).

On "if he touch the uncleanness of man," the witnesses note the defilement. Gill notes the touching of human uncleanness (the defilements of the body, Leviticus 12-15) — the figure (the defilement of sin, the pollution contracted).

On "when he knoweth of it, then he shall be guilty," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the knowing: "when he knoweth of it, then he shall be guilty" — the guilt on knowing (the conscience awakened, the sin acknowledged, vv.5); the figure (the conviction of sin, the guilt felt when known); the providence (the trespass offering when the sin is known, vv.5-6). The verse displays the sin of touching human uncleanness.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the defilement — "if he touch the uncleanness of man... when he knoweth of it, then he shall be guilty," the defilement and knowing (Leviticus 12-15, Keil, Gill); "if he touch the uncleanness of man," the defilement (Gill); "when he knoweth of it, then he shall be guilty," the knowing (vv.5, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the defilement. The verse marks the touching: human uncleanness. The verse displays the hidden: unawares. The verse displays the knowing: the guilt. The verse displays the conviction: awakened. The touching displays the defilement of human uncleanness. The verse displays the contact: defiling. The verse displays the guilt: on knowing. The verse displays the conscience: awakened. The sin of touching human uncleanness displays the defilement on knowing: when he knoweth of it, then he shall be guilty (Leviticus 12-15) — the defilement of human uncleanness, the guilt felt when the sin is known, the conviction needing atonement. The verse displays the rash oath (v.4).
4:
"Or if a soul swear, pronouncing with his lips to do evil, or to do good, whatsoever it be that a man shall pronounce with an oath, and it be hid from him; when he knoweth of it, then he shall be guilty in one of these." The witnesses note the sin of the rash oath. On "if a soul swear, pronouncing with his lips to do evil, or to do good," the grammatical voices note the oath. Calvin and Gill note that "if a soul swear, pronouncing with his lips to do evil, or to do good... and it be hid from him; when he knoweth of it, then he shall be guilty" — if a man swore rashly (a hasty, thoughtless oath, to do evil or good), and forgot it or failed to keep it, when he knew it he was guilty; the rash oath (the hasty, inconsiderate vow, sworn without thought); the evil or good (any rash promise, whether to harm or to benefit); the guilt (the broken or unwise oath, the sin of the tongue, needing atonement).

On "pronouncing with his lips to do evil, or to do good," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the rashness. Calvin and Ellicott note the rash oath (the hasty vow, sworn without thought, perhaps unkept or unwise) — the lesson (the sin of the unbridled tongue, the rash swearing, the danger of hasty vows, Ecclesiastes 5:2-6, "be not rash with thy mouth"; Matthew 5:34-37, "swear not at all"; James 5:12); the warning (against rash oaths and the sins of the tongue).

On "when he knoweth of it, then he shall be guilty," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the guilt: "when he knoweth of it, then he shall be guilty in one of these" — the guilt on knowing (the rash oath acknowledged, the sin of the tongue); the figure (the conviction of the sins of the tongue, James 3:2-8); the providence (the trespass offering for the rash oath, vv.5-6). The verse displays the sin of the rash oath.

The witnesses agree the substance: the rash oath, hastily sworn and then known to be sin, made the man guilty (Calvin, Wesley, Gill). The verse bears its weight: the sin of the rash oath — the hasty, thoughtless vow, sworn without due thought, the sin of the unbridled tongue (Ecclesiastes 5:2-6; James 5:12), the warning against rash swearing ("swear not at all," Matthew 5:34-37), the sins of the tongue needing atonement (James 3:2-8). The verse displays the rash oath. The verse marks the oath: rash. The verse displays the tongue: unbridled. The verse displays the guilt: on knowing. The verse displays the warning: against swearing. The oath displays the sin of the hasty tongue. The verse displays the oath: thoughtless. The verse displays the guilt: real. The verse displays the tongue: to be bridled. The sin of the rash oath displays the sins of the tongue: a rash oath, when he knoweth it, he shall be guilty (Ecclesiastes 5:2-6; Matthew 5:34-37; James 3:2-8) — the hasty, thoughtless vow, the sin of the unbridled tongue, needing atonement. The verse displays confession and the offering (vv.5-6).
5:
"And it shall be, when he shall be guilty in one of these things, that he shall confess that he hath sinned in that thing:" The witnesses note the confession of sin — the crux of confession with the offering. On "when he shall be guilty in one of these things, that he shall confess that he hath sinned," interpreters expound the confession. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the requirement — "he shall confess that he hath sinned in that thing": before bringing the offering, the guilty man was to confess his particular sin; the witnesses note the significance — the confession of sin (the acknowledgment of the specific sin, named and owned, before the atonement); the lesson — the necessity of confession with the sacrifice (no atonement without the owning of sin, the offering not a mechanical rite but joined with the heart's confession; the confession of the particular sin, "I have sinned in this thing"); the figure — the confession of sin the way to forgiveness (Proverbs 28:13, "he that covereth his sins shall not prosper: but whoso confesseth and forsaketh them shall have mercy"; Psalm 32:5, "I acknowledged my sin... and thou forgavest the iniquity of my sin"; 1 John 1:9, "if we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins"); the heart-confession joined with the blood, the figure of repentance and faith in Christ.

On "confess that he hath sinned in that thing," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the particular sin. Wesley and Ellicott note the confession of the particular sin (the specific sin named, not a general confession only) — the particularity (the very sin owned, the honest acknowledgment); the lesson (the confession joined with the offering, the heart with the rite).

On the confession, the grammatical-historical note the acknowledgment: the confession before the offering — the owning of sin; the providence (the confession joined with the sacrifice, the figure of repentance and confession unto forgiveness, 1 John 1:9). The verse displays the confession of sin.

The witnesses agree the substance: the guilty man was to confess his particular sin before bringing the offering (Calvin, Wesley, Ellicott, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of confession with the offering: "he shall confess that he hath sinned" — the necessity of confession with the sacrifice (the particular sin owned and named, the offering joined with the heart's acknowledgment, not a mechanical rite), the way to forgiveness (Proverbs 28:13; Psalm 32:5; 1 John 1:9), the figure of repentance and confession joined with faith in the blood of Christ. The verse displays the confession crux. The verse marks the confession: of sin. The verse displays the sin: owned. The verse displays the offering: joined. The verse displays the forgiveness: the way. The confession displays the owning of sin with the sacrifice. The verse displays the sin: confessed. The verse displays the heart: with the rite. The verse displays the Christ: confession unto him. The confession of sin displays the crux: he shall confess that he hath sinned (Proverbs 28:13; Psalm 32:5; 1 John 1:9) — the confession of the particular sin joined with the offering, the way to forgiveness, the figure of repentance and confession unto Christ. The verse displays the offering for the sin (vv.6-7).
6:
"And he shall bring his trespass offering unto the LORD for his sin which he hath sinned, a female from the flock, a lamb or a kid of the goats, for a sin offering; and the priest shall make an atonement for him concerning his sin." The witnesses note the trespass offering and the atonement for sin. On "he shall bring his trespass offering... a female from the flock, a lamb or a kid of the goats," the grammatical voices note the offering. Calvin and Gill note that "he shall bring his trespass offering unto the LORD for his sin... a female from the flock, a lamb or a kid of the goats, for a sin offering; and the priest shall make an atonement for him" — after confession, the guilty man brought a female lamb or kid as a trespass (sin) offering, and the priest made atonement; the trespass offering (here a sin offering, for the sins named, vv.1-4); the female lamb or kid (the appointed victim); the atonement (the priest making atonement, the covering of sin); the forgiveness (implied, vv.10, "it shall be forgiven him").

On "his trespass offering... for a sin offering," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the offering. Calvin and Ellicott note the trespass offering, here called a sin offering (the offering for the specific trespasses, vv.1-4) — the figure (the one sacrifice for sin, Christ; the offering after confession).

On "the priest shall make an atonement for him concerning his sin," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the atonement: "the priest shall make an atonement for him concerning his sin" — the atonement made (the covering of sin by the blood); the figure (the atonement of Christ, Hebrews 9:22); the providence (the atonement and forgiveness, vv.10; the offering after confession). The verse displays the trespass offering and the atonement for sin.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the offering — "he shall bring his trespass offering... a female from the flock... and the priest shall make an atonement for him," the offering (vv.1-4, Calvin, Gill); "his trespass offering... for a sin offering," the offering (Ellicott, Calvin); "the priest shall make an atonement for him concerning his sin," the atonement (Hebrews 9:22, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the trespass offering. The verse marks the offering: brought. The verse displays the victim: a female lamb. The verse displays the atonement: made. The verse displays the Christ: the atonement. The offering displays the atonement after confession. The verse displays the offering: for sin. The verse displays the atonement: by blood. The verse displays the Christ: our offering. The trespass offering and the atonement for sin display the atonement: he shall bring his trespass offering, and the priest shall make an atonement (Hebrews 9:22) — the offering brought after confession, the atonement made by the blood, the figure of the one sacrifice of Christ. The verse displays the provision for the poor (vv.7-13).
7:
"And if he be not able to bring a lamb, then he shall bring for his trespass, which he hath committed, two turtledoves, or two young pigeons, unto the LORD; one for a sin offering, and the other for a burnt offering." The witnesses note the provision for the poor in the trespass offering — the crux of God's mercy to the poor. On "if he be not able to bring a lamb, then he shall bring... two turtledoves, or two young pigeons," interpreters expound the provision. The dominant reading and the grammatical voices note the mercy — "if he be not able to bring a lamb, then he shall bring... two turtledoves, or two young pigeons": if the offerer was too poor to bring a lamb, he might bring two birds (one for a sin offering, one for a burnt offering); the witnesses note the significance — the gracious provision for the poor (the way of atonement adapted to the offerer's ability, the poor not excluded for want of means); the lesson — God's mercy to the poor (the atonement made accessible to all, the lamb or, if too poor, the birds, or, if poorer still, fine flour, vv.11; none shut out from forgiveness by poverty, the gospel preached to the poor, "the poor have the gospel preached to them," Matthew 11:5; Luke 4:18); the figure — the two birds (Mary's offering at the purification, being poor, Luke 2:24, "a pair of turtledoves, or two young pigeons"; the Lord himself born into a poor family that brought the poor's offering); the accessibility of atonement to all, rich and poor.

On "one for a sin offering, and the other for a burnt offering," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the two birds. Wesley and Ellicott note the two birds (one for sin, one for the burnt offering, the atonement and the consecration) — the figure (the sin borne and the whole devotion, the two aspects of the one atonement); the providence (the poor's offering, the two birds, the atonement for the poor).

On the provision, the grammatical-historical note the mercy: the birds for the poor — the gracious adaptation; the providence (the way of atonement for the poor, God's mercy, the gospel to the poor). The verse displays the provision for the poor in the trespass offering.

The witnesses agree the substance: if too poor for a lamb, the offerer brought two birds (Wesley, Ellicott, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of God's mercy to the poor: "if he be not able to bring a lamb, then he shall bring... two turtledoves" — the gracious provision for the poor (the way of atonement adapted to the offerer's ability, none excluded by poverty), God's mercy to the poor (the gospel preached to the poor, Matthew 11:5; Luke 4:18), the very offering the Lord's own poor parents brought at his purification (Luke 2:24), the accessibility of atonement to all. The verse displays the poor-provision crux. The verse marks the lamb: if unable. The verse displays the birds: for the poor. The verse displays the atonement: accessible. The verse displays the Christ: the poor's offering. The provision displays God's mercy to the poor. The verse displays the poor: provided for. The verse displays the atonement: for all. The verse displays the gospel: to the poor. The provision for the poor in the trespass offering displays the crux: if not able to bring a lamb, two turtledoves (Matthew 11:5; Luke 4:18; 2:24) — the gracious provision for the poor, the atonement accessible to all, the very offering the Lord's poor parents brought. The verse displays the offering of the birds (vv.8-10).
8:
"And he shall bring them unto the priest, who shall offer that which is for the sin offering first, and wring off his head from his neck, but shall not divide it asunder:" The very brief verse notes the offering of the bird for the sin offering. The grammatical voices note the ritual. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "he shall bring them unto the priest, who shall offer that which is for the sin offering first, and wring off his head from his neck, but shall not divide it asunder" — the priest offered the bird for the sin offering first, wringing off its head (pinching the neck) but not dividing the body; the sin offering first (the atonement for sin before the burnt offering of consecration); the wringing off (the killing of the bird, the blood for atonement); the not dividing (the body kept whole); the figure (the sin offering first, the atonement before the devotion, the order of the gospel).

On "who shall offer that which is for the sin offering first," the witnesses note the order. Gill notes the sin offering offered first (the atonement before the burnt offering, vv.7) — the order (the sin dealt with first, then the consecration; the figure, atonement before devotion).

On "wring off his head from his neck, but shall not divide it asunder," the grammatical-historical note the ritual: "wring off his head from his neck, but shall not divide it asunder" — the bird killed by wringing the neck (the blood for atonement), the body not divided; the providence (the poor's sin offering, the atonement for the poor; the figure of the death and atonement). The verse displays the offering of the bird for the sin offering.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the ritual — "the priest... shall offer that which is for the sin offering first, and wring off his head from his neck, but shall not divide it asunder," the sin offering first (vv.7, Keil, Gill); "who shall offer that which is for the sin offering first," the order (Gill); "wring off his head from his neck, but shall not divide it asunder," the ritual (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the bird offering. The verse marks the sin offering: first. The verse displays the bird: killed. The verse displays the order: atonement first. The verse displays the devotion: to follow. The offering displays the atonement before the devotion. The verse displays the sin offering: first. The verse displays the blood: for atonement. The verse displays the order: gospel. The offering of the bird for the sin offering displays the order of atonement: the sin offering first, the head wrung off (vv.7) — the atonement for sin before the burnt offering of consecration, the figure of atonement before devotion. The verse displays the sprinkling of the blood (vv.9-10).
9:
"And he shall sprinkle of the blood of the sin offering upon the side of the altar; and the rest of the blood shall be wrung out at the bottom of the altar: it is a sin offering." The very brief verse notes the sprinkling of the bird's blood. The grammatical voices note the blood. Ellicott and Gill note that "he shall sprinkle of the blood of the sin offering upon the side of the altar; and the rest of the blood shall be wrung out at the bottom of the altar: it is a sin offering" — the priest sprinkled some of the blood on the side of the altar, and the rest was wrung out at the base; the sprinkling (the application of the blood, the atonement); the side of the altar (the blood applied); the wringing out (the rest of the blood at the base, the full giving of the life); the figure (the blood the means of atonement, the life poured out, Christ, Isaiah 53:12).

On "sprinkle of the blood of the sin offering upon the side of the altar," the witnesses note the sprinkling. Gill notes the blood sprinkled on the altar (the atonement applied) — the figure (the blood of Christ, the atonement).

On "the rest of the blood shall be wrung out at the bottom of the altar: it is a sin offering," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the pouring: "the rest of the blood shall be wrung out at the bottom of the altar: it is a sin offering" — the rest of the blood at the base (the full giving of the life); the figure (the life poured out, Isaiah 53:12); the providence (the blood applied and poured, the atonement for the poor). The verse displays the sprinkling of the bird's blood.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the blood — "he shall sprinkle of the blood... upon the side of the altar; and the rest of the blood shall be wrung out at the bottom," the blood (Ellicott, Gill); "sprinkle of the blood of the sin offering upon the side of the altar," the sprinkling (Gill); "the rest of the blood shall be wrung out at the bottom of the altar: it is a sin offering," the pouring (Isaiah 53:12, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the sprinkled blood. The verse marks the blood: sprinkled. The verse displays the altar: the side. The verse displays the pouring: at the base. The verse displays the Christ: poured out. The blood displays the atonement applied. The verse displays the blood: applied. The verse displays the life: poured out. The verse displays the Christ: his soul. The sprinkling of the bird's blood displays the atonement: the blood sprinkled on the altar, the rest at the base (Isaiah 53:12) — the atonement applied and the life poured out, the figure of Christ pouring out his soul unto death even in the poor's offering. The verse displays the burnt offering of the second bird (v.10).
10:
"And he shall offer the second for a burnt offering, according to the manner: and the priest shall make an atonement for him for his sin which he hath sinned, and it shall be forgiven him." The very brief verse notes the burnt offering and the forgiveness for the poor. The grammatical voices note the offering. Clarke and Gill note that "he shall offer the second for a burnt offering, according to the manner: and the priest shall make an atonement for him for his sin which he hath sinned, and it shall be forgiven him" — the second bird was offered for a burnt offering (the consecration), and the priest made atonement, and the sin was forgiven; the burnt offering (the second bird, the whole devotion, after the sin offering); the atonement (the covering of sin by the blood); the forgiveness (the sin forgiven, the poor pardoned as the rich); the figure (the atonement and forgiveness, the poor's full pardon through the offering).

On "he shall offer the second for a burnt offering," the witnesses note the burnt offering. Gill notes the second bird for a burnt offering (the devotion after the atonement) — the figure (the whole given to God after the sin is dealt with).

On "the priest shall make an atonement for him... and it shall be forgiven him," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the forgiveness: "the priest shall make an atonement for him for his sin which he hath sinned, and it shall be forgiven him" — the atonement and forgiveness (the poor pardoned, the same promise as the rich); the figure (the forgiveness through the atonement, Ephesians 1:7); the providence (the poor's atonement and forgiveness, equal with the rich). The verse displays the burnt offering and the forgiveness for the poor.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the offering — "he shall offer the second for a burnt offering... and the priest shall make an atonement for him... and it shall be forgiven him," the offering and forgiveness (Clarke, Gill); "he shall offer the second for a burnt offering," the burnt offering (Gill); "the priest shall make an atonement for him... and it shall be forgiven him," the forgiveness (Ephesians 1:7, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the forgiveness. The verse marks the burnt offering: the second. The verse displays the atonement: made. The verse displays the forgiveness: granted. The verse displays the poor: pardoned. The offering displays the poor's atonement and forgiveness. The verse displays the atonement: by blood. The verse displays the forgiveness: equal. The verse displays the Christ: the atonement. The burnt offering and the forgiveness for the poor display the equal pardon: the priest made atonement, and it was forgiven him (Ephesians 1:7) — the poor's atonement and forgiveness, equal with the rich, through the offering, the figure of forgiveness through Christ. The verse displays the offering of fine flour (vv.11-13).
11:
"But if he be not able to bring two turtledoves, or two young pigeons, then he that sinned shall bring for his offering the tenth part of an ephah of fine flour for a sin offering; he shall put no oil upon it, neither shall he put any frankincense thereon: for it is a sin offering." The witnesses note the fine flour offering for the very poor. On "if he be not able to bring two turtledoves... then he that sinned shall bring... fine flour for a sin offering," the grammatical voices note the provision. Clarke and Gill note that "if he be not able to bring two turtledoves, or two young pigeons, then he that sinned shall bring for his offering the tenth part of an ephah of fine flour for a sin offering" — if too poor even for the birds, the offerer might bring a tenth of an ephah of fine flour for a sin offering; the fine flour (the poorest provision, the cheapest offering, for the very poor); the further mercy (the way of atonement adapted even to the poorest, none excluded); the bloodless offering (the only sin offering without blood, exceptionally allowed for the poorest).

On "fine flour for a sin offering," the reformed and witnesses note the mercy. Gill and Ellicott note the fine flour for the very poor (the cheapest offering, the atonement for the poorest, the further provision) — the lesson (God's mercy reaching even the poorest, none shut out, the atonement accessible to all).

On "he shall put no oil upon it, neither shall he put any frankincense thereon: for it is a sin offering," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the no oil or frankincense: "he shall put no oil upon it, neither... any frankincense" — the absence of oil and frankincense (unlike the ordinary meal offering, Leviticus 2:1) — the witnesses note the reason: it being a sin offering (not a sweet savour of joy and devotion, but for sin; the oil of gladness and the frankincense of acceptance withheld, the solemnity of sin, no rejoicing in the offering for sin); the figure (the sin offering grave, not festal; Christ made sin, the joylessness of bearing sin). The verse displays the fine flour offering for the very poor.

The witnesses agree the substance: if too poor for the birds, the offerer brought fine flour, without oil or frankincense, for a sin offering (Clarke, Wesley, Gill). 12:

14:

15:
"If a soul commit a trespass, and sin through ignorance, in the holy things of the LORD; then he shall bring for his trespass unto the LORD a ram without blemish out of the flocks, with thy estimation by shekels of silver, after the shekel of the sanctuary, for a trespass offering:" The witnesses note the trespass offering for sin in the holy things — the crux of trespass and restitution. On "if a soul commit a trespass, and sin through ignorance, in the holy things of the LORD," interpreters expound the trespass. The reformed and the dominant reading note the holy things — "if a soul commit a trespass... in the holy things of the LORD": if a man trespassed unwittingly in the holy things (withholding tithes, firstfruits, dues, or misusing what was sacred, Leviticus 22:14-16), he was to bring a ram for a trespass offering, with restitution; the witnesses note the significance — the trespass in the holy things (defrauding God of his dues, the sacrilege of withholding or misusing what belongs to him, even unwittingly); the lesson — the guilt of robbing God (the holy things, the tithes and dues, belonging to God, the sin of withholding them, "will a man rob God? yet ye have robbed me... in tithes and offerings," Malachi 3:8); the ram and the estimation (the costly ram, valued in silver by the sanctuary shekel, the trespass requiring both sacrifice and restitution, vv.16); the figure — the trespass against God's holy things requiring both atonement and amends, the satisfaction made.

On "he shall bring... a ram without blemish... with thy estimation by shekels of silver," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the ram and estimation. Wesley and Keil note the ram without blemish, valued in silver (the costly victim and the valuation, the trespass requiring satisfaction) — the figure (the unblemished ram, Christ; the satisfaction made for the wrong).

On the trespass, the grammatical-historical note the holy things: the trespass in the holy things — the robbing of God; the providence (the trespass offering and restitution for the holy things, the wrong against God atoned and restored). The verse displays the trespass offering for sin in the holy things.

16:
"And he shall make amends for the harm that he hath done in the holy thing, and shall add the fifth part thereto, and give it unto the priest: and the priest shall make an atonement for him with the ram of the trespass offering, and it shall be forgiven him." The witnesses note the restitution with the fifth part added. On "he shall make amends for the harm... and shall add the fifth part thereto," the grammatical voices note the restitution. Clarke and Gill note that "he shall make amends for the harm that he hath done in the holy thing, and shall add the fifth part thereto, and give it unto the priest: and the priest shall make an atonement for him... and it shall be forgiven him" — the trespasser made amends (restored what was withheld or misused), adding a fifth part (twenty per cent), and the priest made atonement, and the sin was forgiven; the restitution (the wrong made good, the holy thing restored); the fifth part added (the penalty, the restitution with increase, full satisfaction); the atonement (the ram offered); the forgiveness (the sin forgiven after both restitution and atonement).

On "he shall make amends... and shall add the fifth part thereto," interpreters expound the restitution. The reformed and the dominant reading note the amends — the wrong made good with the added fifth; the witnesses note the lesson — restitution as part of repentance (the wrong not merely confessed but made good, restored with increase, Numbers 5:7; Luke 19:8, Zacchaeus, "if I have taken any thing from any man... I restore him fourfold"); the joining of restitution and atonement (no forgiveness of the wrong without making it good, the offering not excusing the restitution; both the satisfaction to God by the ram and the restitution of the wrong); the lesson for true repentance (the fruit of repentance, the restoring of what was wrongfully taken or withheld).

On "the priest shall make an atonement for him with the ram of the trespass offering, and it shall be forgiven him," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the atonement and forgiveness. 17:
"And if a soul sin, and commit any of these things which are forbidden to be done by the commandments of the LORD; though he wist it not, yet is he guilty, and shall bear his iniquity." The witnesses note the guilt of sin though unknown. On "if a soul sin... though he wist it not, yet is he guilty," interpreters expound the guilt. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the principle — "though he wist it not, yet is he guilty, and shall bear his iniquity": if a man sinned against God's commandments, even without knowing it (unaware that he had sinned, or that the thing was forbidden), he was still guilty and bore his iniquity; the witnesses note the significance — the guilt of sin even when unknown (the sin real and the guilt real, though the sinner was unaware; the objective guilt of sin, not depending on the sinner's knowledge); the lesson — the holiness of God's law and the reality of guilt (sin is sin against God whether known or not, the conscience may be ignorant but the guilt remains, "I know nothing by myself; yet am I not hereby justified," 1 Corinthians 4:4; the secret faults, Psalm 19:12); the need of atonement even for unknown sin (the trespass offering for the sin one does not know, the provision for guilt deeper than the conscience perceives); the figure — the need of Christ's atonement for all sin, even the unknown, the cleansing from secret faults (1 John 1:7).

On "yet is he guilty, and shall bear his iniquity," the wesleyan-arminian and witnesses note the bearing. Wesley and Ellicott note the guilt borne (the iniquity real, the burden until atoned) — the providence (the trespass offering for the unknown sin, vv.18-19); the figure (the need of atonement for guilt unknown).
